w:r.w:t: CHAPTER 1: STARDUST TO STARDUST

A four-room house in Hebron had become the shape of one man's heart, divided against itself — not metaphor but architecture: the western chamber sealed against the eastern, the northern passage choked with seven years of accumulated refusal, each room a record of what remains when grief mistakes itself for patience and patience mistakes itself for virtue. You might ask how a dwelling comes to resemble something as treacherous as a divided heart — such a question, posed while reading this, means you and I will get along. I tell you this as one who was present at that threshold on the night this story begins, and who remained long after the boy inside had gone to places no torch can follow. What was actually happening had been building for seven years, compounding the way a debt compounds: invisibly, then all at once.

Father Aviel had been a scholar, sharp as fresh-knapped flint, sought and summoned by those whose questions twisted beyond their own unraveling. When Mother Zuna passed — nameless there forevermore — something stopped within him: not death, which would have been the cleaner mercy, but the stillness of a cart whose axle has been shattered so completely that no carpenter in the province can name the right repair. The gods he had gathered from the western plains — brittle bronze forms of forgotten flame, of fading fortune, of fertility that promised and did not deliver — could not fill what had gone hollow in him. So he swept them away and kept only one: the lord of limitless dust and earthly plenty, who whispered that hoarded objects might hold back time himself, just to spite whatever kept making new things. That one stayed. That one, as you will discover, was paying close attention all along.

On a night when the moon rode full on its third day, the son moved through the monument his father's madness had made. They had called him Dan once, when names still mattered in this dwelling that hardly stood. He was sixteen, with kind eyes that held more sorrow than a boy should carry and unruly black hair that wrestled with his simple commandments. He had started at the hearth: crouching low, striking flint against pyrite twice before the sparks scattered in a brief gold cascade and the pitch-soaked rag caught, guttering once in protest before it committed. Now the frail flame trembled not from wind but from the staccato percussion against his ribs, casting suspicious shadows across the narrow corridor. Each barefoot step through the refuse was a wager against sound: one shard of pottery, one brittle bone breaking beneath his weight, and the house would know. He was moving toward his father's room, and tonight he would enter the dreamscape to accomplish what no daylight conversation had been able to.

The corridor choked with debris that had long since stopped being merely debris. Broken pottery lay scattered like the shards of failed offerings — Dan had counted eighteen visible pieces one morning before stopping, unable to push past that number. He would not have been able to say why eighteen stopped him. He simply stopped. Fragments of parchment and brittle wax tablets, once bearing records of lineage and covenant, lay stripped clean by mold that had developed what could only be called ambition — spreading in patterns too deliberate for accident, following lines that suggested a map of something no one had commissioned and no one sane would consult. Ruined brass astrolabes gathered in the shadowed corners, their rings seized into arrangements no craftsman had designed, pointing with insistent consistency toward a direction that corresponded to no coordinate in heaven or on earth. Desiccated fruits clung to their seeds in the manner of things that insist they are still alive while offering nothing by way of evidence. From the darkest pocket of the corridor — the corner the torch's light did not quite reach — something made no sound, but in a manner that was different from ordinary silence. The distinction was slight. Dan noticed it the way you notice that a room has someone in it before you find the someone.

The smell came in layers. Ammonia sharp enough to sear the passages and raise involuntary water to the eyes. Decay: sweet and oppressive as overripe fruit three days past its reckoning. Dusty herbs that had surrendered their virtue and become merely powder with the memory of virtue. Maggots worked the rotted cloth in the near dark — pale as mushroom flesh, fat as thumbs — the only living things in this corridor that had found their purpose and intended to keep it, as purposeful as priests at a rite no one had thought to cancel. Flies rose and fell in low, patient clouds; their droning was the liturgy of the house, as established and particular as any psalm. Beneath everything, the mineral reek of disturbed earth, of things buried being pulled back into light where they had no business being. Dan had stopped noticing any of this six years ago. The fact that he noticed it again tonight told him something about the weight of what he was about to attempt.

Here is what the record seldom clarifies, and what you deserve to understand before we go further: some few children come into the world able to remain awake inside the country where all dreamers travel. Not awake the way the body is awake — no muscle moves, no eyelid shifts — but present with the full attention of a watcher who cannot be deceived about what is actually happening. Dan's mother had been such a traveler, and she had begun teaching him when he was still young enough to learn without fully understanding what was being handed to him. To enter another dreamer's country required proximity — the closer the watcher, the cleaner the passage, and this was why Dan moved toward his father's door rather than attempting the work from his own bed. The method was simple enough to describe and requires more discipline than most adults possess: release yesterday, letting it dissolve the way salt dissolves in deep water; release tomorrow, unhooking from its obligations; then release now — this is the difficult one — until the body achieves the stillness of deep water and what is true may surface in it. He had practiced this for five years in secret, in this house. Tonight the practice had a destination.

Dan arrived at his father's door and stopped. He pressed his back to the wall beside it and went still — not the still of patience of serpents in a garden, but the particular stillness of someone listening through stone for a sound they need to hear. From the other side came the sound of a man trying to outrun his own breath: that ragged, straining inhalation belonging to the body running drills for a crisis that has not yet arrived or has long since passed. Dan waited through one long stretch of this, then another. The breath gradually deepened, steadied, lost its urgency. Then, finally — the slow and heavy pull of a man gone fully under. His father was down. He was not running anymore.
He extinguished the torch against the wall.

The dark that arrived was not absence but presence — the darkness that waits behind every flame for exactly this invitation, moving in with the confidence of something that knows the space and has been here before. Dan pressed his palm flat to the doorframe — cold, slightly rough, exactly as solid as stone had always been regardless of what was asked of it — and, barely above a breath, said something to himself. He whispered at the doorframe, “Not ready. Yet here, I am.” Then he closed his eyes.

Released yesterday. Released tomorrow. Released the corridor, the smell, the percussion, the cold coming up through his soles. All released in sequence, the way a rope unwinds one loop at a time from a post. Until the body became still water. Until the dark behind his closed lids changed quality — went from the dark of a closed room to the dark of something vast and formless and older than the thing that named it.
Then, the way a door made of deep water opens, he was through.

The dreamscape received him with the patient depth of a country that has been waiting longer than waiting has a name. The formless dark churned below and the void was total above, as things must have been before the first word of the first morning was spoken into the dark that had not yet known it was waiting. Dan oriented quickly — he knew this country the way a man knows the house he grew up in, could move through it without a lamp — and turned toward his father's presence. He found it immediately: a wall thick and pulsing with old bruise-light, sick and humid, beating with the slow regularity of a man trying not to know something he already knows.

Something bled through that wall that was not Dan's. A child's scream cut short. Blood on white stone. A voice calling one name and stopping mid-syllable. The deep, bottomless shame of a man who cannot locate the moment he should have acted differently. Dan pressed his palm against the wall. Then he pressed his whole intention.

The wall broke — total and sudden as a held breath finally released — and Dan stumbled through into a wasteland beneath an ashen sky, where Father Aviel stood at the plain's center with his shoulders drawn inward and his arms wrapped around himself as if to keep his form, waiting in the posture of a man who has been waiting so long he has forgotten what he waits for. The ground was packed and dry. The sky held no feature.

Dan lifted his palm toward the emptiness above. Intent was enough: in the dreamscape, the body's vocabulary compresses to a single word, and that word is direction. Rocks descended from the motionless sky — not clean rain but hard mineral chunks, angular and indifferent. Aviel looked up. One struck his shoulder. He picked it up, and in the peculiar logic of the place it transformed in his hand — became gold, dense and cold, carrying the specific weight of what men will abandon their families for. His eyes went wide. More fell, each transforming: copper pieces, silver rings, gems holding colors too deep and too bright for anything belonging to daylight. Aviel began to gather with the frantic efficiency of a man who has been waiting his entire life for exactly this permission. The pile rose past his ankles, past his knees, past his waist, and still his hands would not stop trying to fill something with no floor.

Past his chest. Past his neck. Aviel was buried to the chin with his hands frozen mid-grasp, his fingers fused to the metal, his face cycling through dark red and then the deep purple of something being pressed closed from outside. His mouth opened in a silent accusation at the empty sky.

Dan held the image. Then raised his palm a final time. The wind that came was cold and total: it gathered everything into a spiral and scattered it all back into dust, and the dust dissolved as it fell. The plain emptied. Aviel knelt alone with his hands still shaped for holding. For one moment his eyes cleared — he looked up, and above him appeared a single star, small and cold as the truest things are. He stared at it with the expression of a man discovering he has been forgiven for something he never confessed. Something in him cracked — not broke entirely, but cracked enough to admit one thin shaft of light. Dan released his hold. The star faded. The plain dissolved. Aviel drifted down toward the deep current of dreamless sleep, and Dan let him go. From behind his father's door came the sound of a man sleeping deeply — the slow, unhurried draw of someone no longer running. Dan stood still and listened to it for a long moment. Then he kept on forward..

Dan gasped back into the dark corridor, stone against his knees, the smell a solid wall. The torch was out. He pressed one hand to the wall and began moving — feet testing each step, his body heavier than it had been, his legs uncertain beneath him, the cold of the floor working up through his soles with the thorough patience of cold that has been held in stone all night. He moved by feel until the warmth of the banked coals met his face before his eyes could find the glow. He touched the pitch to ember. The torch caught. He stood for a moment while his world spun slightly, holding the returned light.

Consider, before I describe what comes next, that there is nothing remarkable about a clean room. People have them everywhere. What is remarkable — what I watched for six years particularly near various preferred corners of this house, as I made a particular study of the boy — was a clean room maintained by deliberate act inside a house that was actively trying to become something else. While the western passage filled with crates and the eastern room became a storage argument the crates were losing, while the corridor between accumulated its purposeful mold and its astrolabes pointing toward nothing good, Dan's room had been maintained with a precision that spoke not of tidiness but of something closer to decision. The blanket lay folded with crisp corners. The floor was swept clean. His possessions arranged with careful economy: the clay cup, the wooden comb his mother had carved from a fig branch, the flint stylus for marking stone, and one small window kept clear so that morning light could enter, which it did, daily, whether or not anyone stood in it to receive it. He had maintained this order since the age of ten. Without instruction, without acknowledgment, without any particular expectation of reward. One might note — and I do note — that Dan had spent six years putting his own house in order before he ever dared concern himself with the disorder of the world immediately outside his door. The room was the hard-won proof of it. He would not have used those words. The room was there regardless, and it was ordered, and it mattered in ways he did not yet have language for.

His shadow however, projected on a wall by the active flame, was hunched and wrong — the silhouette of a much older exhaustion compressed into a sixteen-year-old frame. He did not see it. He lowered himself onto the cot, pressed the torch into its crack in the wall — the flicker burning low but not yet done, not yet willing to concede — and lay back. Barely shaped into words, he said something to the ceiling stone that ended with "Mother" and contained the word "stardust." The ceiling stone did not respond. He had not asked it to. The exhaustion took him without announcement, the way a tide takes what the shore has been holding: without particular violence, but with the absolute certainty of a process that has simply decided it is time.
What came was not rest. It was the sleep that seizes the body when the body has stopped asking the will for permission.

Dan found himself standing on water — bare feet on a surface that was liquid at one angle and solid at another, the way certain truths hold only when approached directly. The sky above held a blue found only in the country where waking and sleeping share a border — not a color belonging to daylight, carrying no equivalent in any mineral or dye or season. For a moment the stillness of the place offered something close to peace. He took one step. The water held. He took another.

Then it changed beneath him. Thickened and went clear — not water anymore but something harder and translucent, cold as polished obsidian and black at depth. He could see down through it into distances that had no floor, and shapes moved below with the unhurried deliberation of things that have all of time. Then he heard it: a single crystalline snap.

Fracture lines raced outward from beneath his feet with the velocity of certainty. The sound built from whisper to roar in the span of three slow breaths — and then the floor of the world exploded upward.

The wave hit him like a wall collapsing. He went under at once, the cold driving through every layer of him, and when he surfaced — choking, thrashing — the sky above was no longer blue. It had gone the color of a wound: deep purple bleeding to absolute black at the edges, clouds assembling into shapes that moved with intent. The sea churned white at the crests and black in the troughs, and the foam at his lips tasted of brine and something beneath brine — mineral, ancient, absolute — making his teeth ache to the root.
The light in the water below changed.

Something was ascending from directly underneath him, and the sea above it went white from the force of the pressure. The water shoved Dan back. The waves became mountains. And then the thing broke the surface. It did not arrive all at once. This is important. This is how it comes.

First: a forehead. Immense, streaming, the color of drowned stone. Bearing the marks of eons of pressure — not damage, but record. Ridge and compression and deep-set line, the way a cliff face bears its strata across a hundred generations of slow testimony. Then the eyes: wide as wells, black rim to rim, without reflection, without the movement that living eyes carry — eyes that had been open in the dark longer than memory and had long since ceased to require anything from the light. Then the rest assembling itself below: barnacled cheeks, the jaw broad as a city gate, seaweed draping the chin like the vestments of something that had its own priests before the first priest drew breath. No expression moved in that face. That was the detail that made the waves seem ordinary by comparison. It regarded him with the complete patience of stone regarding weather.

And far to the south — too far to see but known with the certainty that belongs only to dreams — a massive tail broke the surface, spanning horizon to horizon, and the ridges it displaced rolled outward in long, slow pulses like the breathing of something for which the word *heartbeat* would be a category error.
The voice arrived from inside makeup of Dan's skull.

"I am Dagon — Lord of Abundance Manifest, Master of Accumulation Eternal." Not sound exactly: a vibration that began at the roots of the teeth and moved inward, through the jaw, through the sternum, through the long bones into the dark below them. "This night have you sinned most grievously, daring to undo what I established within your father's dwelling through seven years of deliberate cultivation. Do not mistake your small, transient motion for a living offering worthy of divine consideration."
Dan got a mouthful of dark water and spat past the vision's edge. "I did not call you."

"Your flesh calls ceaselessly. It seeks its appointed rest." The eyes did not blink. "Come into the comfortable depths. I shall restore your mother's voice to you in dream — her comfort unceasing. A wife with unreddened hands. Sons who will not know their grandfather's shame. The slow warmth of a life from which the cruelties of the heights have been removed."

The vision did not announce itself — it pressed in through the long bones, through the marrow, through the specific ache of something the body recognizes before the mind has finished refusing it. A house with lamps lit. A woman at a low table. Laughter from past an open door. A life from which the sharpest things had been carefully, gently removed. The pull registered the way a deep well registers in a man standing too close to the edge — not imagined, not symbolic, but the body's own weight understood suddenly as the beginning of a fall. Something in Dan's chest answered it before any other part of him could intervene.
His hand found the stone at his chest. His fingers closed around it.
"Your temple fell to a blinded man," he said. "You crumbled before the Sacred Chest."

Something moved through the vast face — not expression, nothing that simple. A reorganization. "My house fell not for want of strength, boy. There exist boundaries ancient and immutable. The living cannot enter the realm of the dead without consequence. What you name failure, I name discipline — restraint practiced across more ages than your language has numbers for. Do not mistake the patience of ages for the failure of a moment."
"Then you'll even admit it. You are a Being of Death!"

"Look! War rages within you." The eyes lowered. Not to Dan. To the sea. To the shapes moving in the depth far below. "Look at the battles between men. Death is not a distant shore. It is the very sea in which you are standing at this moment."
"Why should I serve you and not what holds higher?"

"The one who pursues the heights becomes the monument isolated — cold, absolute, and alone." The eyes rose, and something entered them that was not anger and was worse than anger. "You will ascend, and your tongue will become a heavy thing past the use of it. What you speak will be received as the noise of chaff the wind drives away. You will have no witness. I tell you this plainly, while the choice remains: knock three times, ask twice, wait. I will come with certainty absolute. I am the cadence you are attempting to outrun. You only choose when we play."
Dan got a full mouthful of the sea and let it run back out. "I watched what your comfort bought my father. I would drink this sea before living one day in your filth."

Dagon laughed — the low, foundational sound of an ocean deciding on a new shore: absolutely certain it will arrive. "Brave little Being, however borrowed." The eyes did not close. The sea stilled into an enormous and particular silence. "Very well."
The water closed over him.

Dan woke with salt in his throat — not dream-salt, or not only dream-salt, but sweat: the body's own brine, soaking his tunic flat against his chest, the frantic furnace within announcing itself against his sternum. He lay flat, running the inventory: the clean-swept floor, the possessions in their order, the torch burning low and stubborn in its crack in the wall, the window showing the sky at the earliest possible stage of deciding whether it might eventually become morning.

He rose and went to the window. Hebron's rooftops lay below in a darkness that had barely begun to reconsider itself. From above, stars looked on with timeless indifference. He pressed his palm to the stone sill — cold working into his palm slowly, thoroughly — and stood while words surfaced in him the way the deepest things surface: not sought, simply there.

"Stardust," he said — to himself, or to the sill, or to the dark above the city; the distinction was not one he could make, let alone myself. His voice came out smaller than intended. "She said we're made of it. A pause. "Tell me it means something."
The sky above Hebron did not answer. He had not expected it to.

He returned to the cot and lay back. The little torch in its crack burned lower still, the flame faltered to a patient coal-colored failure, in no apparent hurry to go out but clearly coming to a decision. He pulled the thin blanket to his chin and lay listening to the house settle in its old arguments — wood contracting in the night cold, something in the northern passage shifting with the sound of a thought reconsidered too late. The flatness that settled into him was not peace and not rest but the total evacuation of further capacity: the specific texture of effort too large and too private to be witnessed by anyone who was not present for it. His eyes closed.

What came was not restful — the record is clear on this — any more than the sleep his father was currently inhabiting behind a debris-choked corridor wall was restful. They were sleeping the same sleep now, father and son: the sleep of men who have each spent the night on something that mattered, and are not yet sure whether it mattered enough.

I must tell you — and I say this as one who has been waiting in corners for considerably longer than you might imagine, watching this particular family with the attention of a watcher who cannot be fooled — that what came next arrived as two things at once. Not one. Two. Both in the same silence, both with the same patience, with entirely different intentions.

From below the house — beneath the foundation stone, beneath the packed soil, from wherever the roots of things descend past the reach of any lamp — a grinding. Slow and dense and absolute, the specific sound of something that knows exactly where it is and has no urgency whatsoever about what comes next. It did not knock. Did not call. It simply became more present, the way a shape assembles in a dark room when the eye finally stops avoiding it: not arriving but becoming unmistakable. It carried in it, like an object pressed into wet clay, the impression of a voice and a phrase that voice had left behind.

And at the door of the clean and ordered room — at the threshold Dan had maintained against the encroachment of everything outside it — a sound so different from the first that they might have come from different worlds, which both is and is not, in the most important sense, the case. Not quite rapping, not quite tapping: the investigation of something curious enough to inquire and wise enough to wait. A late visitor entreating entrance at the chamber's lattice.. Warm in a manner the grinding below was not. Warm as a coal kept burning in an empty room, on the chance that someone might eventually return.
(A glorious way to describe the two presences on top of what it already is, is it describe the Earth sounds as roaring and the one in treating entrance at his chamber lattice, as a gentle purr…)

[[[I'm providing also the previous draft so you can see some of the changes I made for example: used “patience” way too much. I've altered it a few times and there's still too many. Also the word absolute, so I have to redo the lexical library and search for any mistakes across any of and all of the protocol and all bit of information within them. Also I changed that the two came from the same world the presences, you both is and is not off the signal because the dualism and parallelism has to still respect the whole. Except that it is I guess in the case of the “hole” which isn't a delusional state…]]]
Two presences. One at the door. One below the floor.
The torch gave its last.
“No witness,”said the dark.

(The personification of the dark is good here but I think it can be said without using the word dark because it makes it cheap. Perhaps something that shows the dark and the lightest two sides at the same coin? Cuz that would respect the whole again)
